title: Superposition and polysemanticity
learningObjectives: Explain superposition and polysemanticity in plain language
Write down the objective and identify its assumptions
Design one test that could falsify an over-strong interpretation

whyThisLesson.text: Superposition allows more features than dimensions by placing feature directions non-orthogonally. A neuron can then respond to several unrelated features. This lesson gives you a concrete test for that claim before the course moves to the next abstraction.

intuition.analogy: Treat a transformer as a busy workshop with a shared workbench. Each component adds or reads a mark, but a mark is not an explanation until changing it changes the job's outcome. Superposition allows more features than dimensions by placing feature directions non-orthogonally. A neuron can then respond to several unrelated features.
intuition.analogyLimit: A descriptive feature label is not a circuit. The explanation must survive causal intervention and negative controls.
intuition.text: Superposition allows more features than dimensions by placing feature directions non-orthogonally. A neuron can then respond to several unrelated features.

toyExample.description: Choose a small behaviour, name a candidate feature path, then ablate or patch it while holding the prompt family fixed.

steps.result: Specify exactly what superposition and polysemanticity should recover, preserve, or ignore.
steps.insight: The intended factor must be known before a toy result can be judged.

steps.result: Use the same data, capacity, training budget, and evaluation examples for both methods.
steps.insight: Otherwise a visible gain may be a resource difference in disguise.

steps.result: Vary the source correlation, noise, view quality, or sparsity and record the first failure.
steps.insight: The boundary of success is usually more useful than the headline score.

visual.question: Which assumption behind superposition and polysemanticity changes the result most?
visual.description: Move one control at a time. Treat the display as a toy model, then write down the real experiment it suggests.

technical.text: Superposition allows more features than dimensions by placing feature directions non-orthogonally. A neuron can then respond to several unrelated features. In practice, write down the input unit, the operation applied to it, and the exact statistic used to score the result. Those are different things. A learned representation can be useful even when the interpretation attached to it turns out to be wrong.

mathematics.explanation: For this lesson, the expression makes the central trade-off explicit. Superposition allows more features than dimensions by placing feature directions non-orthogonally. A neuron can then respond to several unrelated features.
mathematics.whyNeeded: It says precisely what the method is being rewarded for.
mathematics.ifRemoved: You could not tell whether two implementations were solving the same problem.
mathematics.encourages: A testable prediction about the chosen objective.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: A descriptive feature label is not a circuit. The explanation must survive causal intervention and negative controls.

methodComparison.question: What kind of claim is made?
methodComparison.practicalImplication: Only the last tests whether the component contributes to the behaviour.

evidence.claim: Superposition allows more features than dimensions by placing feature directions non-orthogonally. A neuron can then respond to several unrelated features.
evidence.note: Course explanation. Use the research library's primary sources for paper-specific empirical claims.

failureModes.name: A score that answers the wrong question
failureModes.mechanism: The objective rewards a proxy while the paper describes a stronger scientific property.
failureModes.symptom: The headline metric rises, but seed stability, controls, or interventions disagree.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Add a matched negative control and one counterfactual intervention.
failureModes.mitigation: Either narrow the claim or add the missing measurement.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Report both the intended benefit and the most plausible shortcut for every SetConCA loss term.

setconcaConnection.relevance: Superposition and polysemanticity determines how SetConCA should represent, group, or evaluate activation views.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The choice of sparse code, view set, coordination loss, or evaluation.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Match the pointwise SAE and SetConCA on model, activation site, width, sparsity budget, and compute.
setconcaConnection.whichFailure: Mistaking stronger agreement for recovery of the intended concept.
setconcaConnection.whichExperiment: Compare fidelity, feature stability, selectivity, and controlled interventions; report each result separately.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: An apparent gain may come from extra context, effective capacity, or easier positives rather than set coordination.

exercises.prompt: A paper claims that superposition and polysemanticity improved interpretability. What one result would you ask for before accepting that claim?
exercises.reveal: Ask for the smallest missing control: a matched baseline, several seeds, a negative control, or an intervention that tests the claimed mechanism.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Explain superposition and polysemanticity to a colleague, then name one thing the method cannot establish by itself.

nextLesson.bridge: Next: SAEs as overcomplete dictionaries. The next idea reuses this lesson's representation and makes a new assumption explicit.

fields.label: Falsifiable claim
fields.prompt: Write the smallest claim this lesson supports.

fields.label: Control
fields.prompt: What control would rule out a shortcut?
